briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 2, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Extreme Weather Events and Threats to Food Security
sections.content: Today, severe climate phenomena caused by climate change and their associated threats have been extensively reported both domestically and internationally. Domestically, disruptions have occurred in the supply of Sumi potatoes, which typically grow well even in poor harvest years. Furthermore, warnings for national park closures due to heatwaves indicate a deepening complex crisis in the climate and energy sectors [1, 2, 5, 6]. Internationally, the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) has warned of the imminent possibility of a strong El Niño phenomenon, stating that climate change will further exacerbate its impacts [32, 36, 52, 54, 55, 58, 59, 63]. Notably, record-high sea temperatures were observed in Spain in May, further amplifying concerns about climate change [42, 51].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Efforts in Climate Change Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Amidst these crises, positive domestic and international efforts to respond to climate change and achieve carbon neutrality continue. In Jeongeup City, Jeollabuk-do, the full-scale cultivation of 'Dalhami,' a new rice variety resilient to climate change, has begun. In urban gardens in Seoul, lettuce and basil capable of withstanding heatwaves are being grown [3, 4, 8]. Yeongcheon City and Ulsan have launched academies and educational programs for children to address abnormal climate change, thereby enhancing adaptive capacity [7, 9]. Furthermore, Gwangmyeong City is pursuing a green transition beyond redevelopment to become a carbon-neutral city, and the UK government has set a target to reduce carbon emissions by 87% by 2042. These examples illustrate the global spread of practical and policy efforts towards carbon neutrality [16, 17, 22, 23, 50, 61, 66].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic and International Expansion and Challenges of Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is a key driver for achieving carbon neutrality and is being actively pursued both domestically and internationally. The Korea Industrial Complex Corporation has signed a business agreement to expand renewable energy supply in industrial complexes in the Daegu region. In industrial complexes in Gyeonggi and Chungcheong provinces, the expansion of solar power utilizing idle land is being promoted [27, 28, 29]. In the aviation industry, the mandatory use of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) and the introduction of production tax credits are being discussed, accelerating progress towards carbon neutrality goals [18, 20]. However, the United States is also facing political and economic challenges in the renewable energy transition process, as evidenced by six states filing lawsuits against the Trump administration's attempt to cancel offshore wind power project lease agreements [65].

sections.title: ⚠️ Widespread Social Impacts of Climate Change and International Responses
sections.content: Climate change extends beyond mere environmental issues, exerting widespread impacts across society, including altering the causes of war and exacerbating religious conflicts [10, 43]. In the United States, climate change is directly affecting daily life in local communities, with growing concerns over harmful algal blooms in Alaska and being identified as a factor preventing California lawyers from returning to work [44, 46]. Furthermore, a report indicates that half of the insurance premium increases since 2008 have been caused by climate change [62]. More concerning is the news that the United States plans to dismantle a crucial ocean observation system previously used to study the impacts of climate change in the Atlantic, which could significantly hinder the acquisition of essential data for climate change response [40, 64].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the multifaceted threats posed by climate change, along with various domestic and international efforts to overcome them, and the challenges that still remain. Sustained attention and international cooperation are becoming increasingly crucial to overcome the climate crisis. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Dismay as Trump officials to dismantle key ocean monitoring system
articles.description: Ocean Observatories Initiative, $368m network that has provided crucial climate data, latest victim of Trump cutsThe Trump administration plans to dismantle a $368m deep-sea observation system that has for more than a decade provided crucial data on ocean systems and climate change.In a notice, the National Science Foundation (NSF) announced that it had “initiated descoping of the Ocean Observatories Initiative” (OOI), a vast ocean observation network comprising more than 900 instruments that co

summary.phenomenon: The Trump administration plans to dismantle the Ocean Observatories Initiative (OOI), a $368 million deep-sea observation system that has provided crucial data on ocean systems and climate change for over a decade. This system comprises more than 900 instruments.
summary.cause: The Ocean Observatories Initiative (OOI) is slated for dismantling due to budget cuts by the Trump administration.
summary.outlook: The provision of crucial climate change data may be discontinued.

articles.title: Six US states sue Trump administration over deal to kill windfarm project
articles.description: State attorneys general argue $1bn deal to terminate major offshore wind lease off the coast of New York is unlawfulSix states sued the Trump administration on Tuesday over its decision to cancel a major offshore wind lease off the coast of New York.In March, federal officials announced they would pay nearly $1bn in taxpayer dollars to French energy firm TotalEnergies in exchange for the company killing plans to erect two offshore windfarms off New York and North Carolina. TotalEnergies agreed t

summary.phenomenon: Six U.S. states filed a lawsuit on Tuesday against the Trump administration's decision to pay approximately $1 billion in taxpayer money to the French energy firm TotalEnergies to terminate a major offshore wind lease off the coast of New York. This agreement was intended to halt plans to construct two offshore wind farms off the coasts of New York and North Carolina.
summary.cause: The Trump administration entered into a $1 billion agreement with TotalEnergies to halt offshore wind power projects.
summary.outlook: State attorneys general are arguing that this agreement is unlawful, and future legal disputes are anticipated.

TotalEnergies agreed t

articles.title: New ways to remove CO2 from atmosphere must grow much faster, report says
articles.description: Novel forms of CO2 removal must expand at ‘highly ambitious rates’ if world is to limit global heating to 1.5C, says studyHumanity must suck carbon out of the atmosphere with new technologies even faster than the breakneck speed with which it has deployed solar panels if it is to limit global heating to 1.5C, a report has found.Novel forms of carbon dioxide removal (CDR) must grow at “highly ambitious rates” to bridge the gap between what governments have pledged to clean up and what is needed t

summary.phenomenon: According to a report, if the world is to limit global warming to 1.5°C, new technologies for removing atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) must expand at a pace significantly faster than the deployment of solar panels.
summary.cause: This is due to a gap between governments' pledged carbon reduction commitments and the actual reductions needed to achieve the 1.5°C global warming limit.
summary.outlook: It is projected that achieving the 1.5°C global warming limit will be challenging unless novel forms of Carbon Dioxide Removal (CDR) technologies grow at highly ambitious rates.

articles.original_description: Novel forms of CO2 removal must expand at ‘highly ambitious rates’ if world is to limit global heating to 1.5C, says studyHumanity must suck carbon out of the atmosphere with new technologies even faster than the breakneck speed with which it has deployed solar panels if it is to limit global heating to 1.5C, a report has found.Novel forms of carbon dioxide removal (CDR) must grow at “highly ambitious rates” to bridge the gap between what governments have pledged to clean up and what is needed t

articles.original_title: How England’s largest forest went from commodity to conservation haven
articles.description: Kielder in Northumberland is balancing commercial production with conserving peatland and rare plants and animalsDriving through part of Northumberland, you might look around at the tall Sitka spruce and imagine yourself in Canada’s evergreen forests, or perhaps, on a sunny day, in northern California. Instead, you are in England’s largest forest, Kielder, often heralded as a success story that balances commercial production with ambitious conservation.The first trees of this 60,000-hectare fore
